FT_IC1.evidence: US-ABCD Release 3.0 baseline data, N=9,738 (9-10 year olds)

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA): typical weekday and weekend SMA time measured as hours; qualifies as smartphone/screen time per B7

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effect regression models testing association of brain structural covariation pattern with SMA; coefficients reported for SMA→externalizing pathway

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients: SMA β=0.107, P=0.002; externalizing psychopathology β=0.117, P=0.002 (reported in abstract and main text)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis using JIVE approach to identify brain structural covariation patterns and test associations with SMA and outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article (Journal of Behavioral Addictions 2022); no evidence of preprint/abstract/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SMA is independent variable (predictor), not dependent variable. Paper analyzes SMA→brain structure/externalizing behavior, not predictors of SM use. Does not meet condition (a) of FT-EC7.
notes: Clear empirical analysis of SM exposure predicting brain structure and externalizing outcomes in US-ABCD sample. All inclusion criteria met.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study baseline data, N=11,834 children ages 9-10, US-based

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time activity (SMA) included as predictor in mixed-effects model; qualifies as smartphone/screen time variable per B7

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression and mixed-effects model with associations between p factor and predictor variables (screen time activity, sleep duration, physical activity, household income, gender)

FT_IC4.evidence: Paper reports only aggregate variance explained (8.02% for all predictors combined) with no individual coefficients for screen time extracted. Per B2 rule: summed/total effect reported, no per-item breakdown of SM coefficient separately.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis conducting confirmatory factor analysis on ABCD baseline cohort data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published conference proceedings (IEEE BIBM 2021); no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is predictor/IV (not dependent variable as DV); mixed with non-demographic predictors (sleep, physical activity); cross-sectional design. Does not meet condition (a) or (b) of FT-EC7.
notes: FT-IC4 fails because individual screen time coefficient not reported separately from aggregate model variance. Paper combines screen time with sleep, physical activity, and demographics in single mixed-effects model reporting only combined R² without per-predictor estimates.

FT_IC1.evidence: Describes US-ABCD study (nearly 12,000 youth ages 9-10 from across United States)

FT_IC2.evidence: Mentions 'screen media use' only as example of data collected in COVID-19 pandemic follow-ups; no specific qualifying smartphone or social media variable analyzed; no empirical coefficient reported for any SM measure

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential tests of SM associations. Commentary describes ABCD data collection methods and opportunities; no hypothesis testing or statistical analysis presented

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric SM-related coefficients, p-values, effect sizes, or correlations reported

FT_IC5.evidence: Commentary/perspective article per B14; describes ABCD study as resource and discusses future research opportunities rather than presenting original empirical analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Published article (Journal of Adolescent Health 2022); not a duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper does not meet FT-IC2 through FT-IC5 criteria; FT-EC7 not applicable to papers failing inclusion criteria. Classified as commentary/perspective (resource paper) per B14.
notes: EXCLUDE: Commentary/perspective article describing ABCD study design, data collection, and research opportunities. Does not report original empirical SM analyses. Multiple IC criteria not met (FT-IC2, FT-IC3, FT-IC4, FT-IC5).
